title: What Are Certification Badges? How They Work and How to Issue Them
excerpt: Certification badges are verifiable digital credentials that prove a skill or qualification. Learn how they work, the open standards behind them, and how to issue them.
content_html:
Certification badges are verifiable digital credentials that prove someone has earned a specific certification, completed a course, or demonstrated a skill. Unlike a static PDF or a printed certificate, the badge carries tamper-evident metadata — who issued it, what it represents, when it was earned, and how to verify it — so anyone can confirm it is genuine in seconds.

As hiring and compliance move online, these credentials have become a standard way for training providers, universities, and employers to recognise achievement in a format that is portable, shareable, and practically impossible to fake.

How certification badges work

A badge looks like an image, but the real value sits in the data attached to it. Each one bundles structured metadata that follows an open standard, typically including:

  • Issuer — the organisation that awarded it, with a verifiable identity.
  • Recipient — the person who earned it, often with privacy controls.
  • Criteria — exactly what the recipient had to do to earn it.
  • Evidence and dates — supporting proof, the issue date, and any expiry.
  • A verification link — so a third party can confirm the badge is authentic and unaltered.

Because the credential is cryptographically signed, it cannot be edited after issuing without breaking verification. That is what separates a real digital credential from a screenshot of one.

Certification badges vs. paper certificates

Traditional certificates were designed for a paper world. These credentials are designed for the web, which changes everything about how they are trusted and used:

  • Verifiable — anyone can confirm authenticity instantly, with no phone calls or emails.
  • Portable — recipients add them to LinkedIn, email signatures, and résumés in one click.
  • Data-rich — the achievement, criteria, and evidence travel with the badge.
  • Fraud-resistant — forged or expired credentials are easy to detect.

If you are weighing formats, our guide to what digital badges are breaks down the wider family of badge types and shows where a certification badge fits.

The open standards that make them trustworthy

Most are built on open standards so they work across platforms instead of locking you into a single vendor. The two that matter most are Open Badges — the 1EdTech specification that defines the badge metadata — and verifiable credentials, the W3C standard for cryptographically signed, tamper-evident credentials. A modern certification badge often combines both: the Open Badges structure for portability, and verifiable-credential signing for trust. Choosing a format built on these standards means your credentials stay valid and verifiable even if you change tools later.

How to issue a certification badge

Issuing them is straightforward once you choose a platform. The typical workflow is:

  1. Define the credential — name, description, criteria, and any expiry.
  2. Design the artwork — a clear visual that reflects your brand and the achievement.
  3. Connect your data — import recipients from your LMS, CRM, or a spreadsheet.
  4. Issue and notify — recipients receive a link to claim and share what they earned.
  5. Let anyone verify — each badge gets a public verification page proving it is genuine.

Good platforms let you issue in bulk, so awarding hundreds or thousands of certification badges after an exam or training cohort takes minutes rather than days.

Why they matter for your organisation

For training providers and employers, these credentials turn completed programmes into a trust and marketing asset. Every time a recipient shares a badge, it links back to your verification page and your brand, extending your reach at no extra cost. They also cut administrative load: instead of fielding "can you confirm this person is certified?" emails, you point verifiers to a self-service page. And because the data is structured, you gain analytics on issuance, sharing, and engagement that paper credentials could never provide.

Choosing a platform

The right platform depends on volume, branding control, standards support, and how easily recipients can verify and share. We compare the leading options — including pricing and standards support — in our guide to the best digital credentialing platforms. Look for native Open Badges and verifiable-credential support, bulk issuing, and a verification experience that works without recipients needing to create an account.

Frequently asked questions

Are certification badges the same as digital badges?

A certification badge is a type of digital badge focused specifically on proving a certification, qualification, or completed assessment. All certification badges are digital badges, but digital badges also include participation, membership, and skill badges that may not certify a formal credential.

Can a badge expire?

Yes. Many certifications are time-limited, so a badge can carry an expiry date in its metadata. When it lapses, the verification page reflects that automatically — far more reliable than a paper certificate that gives no sign it is out of date.

How does someone verify one?

Every badge includes a verification link to a public page hosted by the issuer or platform. That page confirms the issuer, the criteria, the issue and expiry dates, and that the credential has not been altered since it was issued — no login required.
